skill.name: web-research
skill.description: Guides structured web research by planning subtopics, delegating searches to subagents, saving findings, and synthesizing cited reports.
skill.summary: Plan, delegate, and synthesize web research with saved findings and citations.
skill.author: langchain-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported Ruby backtick execution and weak cryptography, but the reviewed file is Markdown guidance and no executable Ruby or cryptographic implementation is present. The real risk is operational: the skill directs web-enabled subagents to gather untrusted web content and write research artifacts to local files, so publication should include a warning.

medium_findings.title: Web-Enabled Subagents Process Untrusted Sources
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs research subagents to use web_search and fetch_url for current web content. This is legitimate for research, but untrusted pages can contain misleading content or prompt injection that may affect synthesis if not handled carefully.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly grants subagents web_search access and references fetch_url for web pages. The risk is contextual rather than malicious because web access is central to the skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: Local File Writes During Research Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents and subagents to create folders and write research plans, findings, and optional reports. This is expected behavior, but it can modify the workspace and should be constrained to the requested research folder.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions repeatedly require write_file and saved findings. The behavior is legitimate but creates filesystem impact in the user workspace.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across SKILL.md, but the reviewed content uses Markdown code fences, inline tool names, and a simple mkdir example. No executable Ruby code or dynamic command execution is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review confirms Markdown guidance, tool references, and one directory creation example rather than executable code. The static pattern is explained by backticks used for formatting.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Detections Are Description False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged weak cryptographic algorithms at the metadata description and overview. Those lines describe comprehensive web research and do not contain hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain only descriptive prose. No MD5, SHA1, cipher selection, or credential handling appears in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Delegated Tool Use With Web and File Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow combines task-based delegation, web_search, and write_file. This pattern is useful for research but increases the chance that untrusted source text or broad subagent instructions affect local outputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly documented in the skill. It is not inherently malicious, but it has a meaningful risk surface because delegated agents can search the web and write files.

content.value_statement: Open-ended research can become scattered and hard to verify. This skill creates a plan, delegates focused web searches, saves findings, and synthesizes cited answers.
actual_capabilities: Creates a dedicated research folder for plans, findings, and reports.
Breaks broad research questions into two to five focused subtopics.
Delegates subtopic research to task subagents with web_search access.
Limits each subagent to three to five web searches for scope control.
Reads saved findings and synthesizes a cited final answer.
Optionally writes a final research report when requested.
limitations: Requires web_search, task, read_file, write_file, list_files, and fetch_url tools to be useful.
Does not verify source truth beyond the sources gathered during the research run.
Can create local files in the active workspace during normal use.
May miss information if subtopic planning is incomplete or search results are limited.

use_cases.title: Compare Vendor Options
use_cases.description: Research features, pricing signals, public documentation, and tradeoffs across several products.

use_cases.title: Brief Current Market Trends
use_cases.description: Gather recent web sources and synthesize a concise trend report with cited evidence.

use_cases.title: Prepare Technical Background Research
use_cases.description: Investigate frameworks, APIs, or implementation approaches before making a technical plan.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Topic Research
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a concise researched answer.
prompt_templates.prompt: Research the current state of [topic]. Create a short plan, gather recent sources, and summarize the key findings with URLs.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Three Options
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate comparison across products, tools, or approaches.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare [option A], [option B], and [option C] for [decision context]. Research each option separately, then synthesize strengths, risks, and best fit.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Evidence Brief
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a sourced business or policy brief.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an evidence brief on [question]. Split the work into focused subtopics, save findings, cite sources, and identify gaps or uncertainty.

prompt_templates.title: Research Report With Saved Artifacts
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow that needs a reusable report file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Research [complex topic] and write a final report. Save the research plan, subtopic findings, and final report in a dedicated research folder.

output_examples.input: Research recent adoption trends for serverless databases.
output_examples.output: A structured answer summarizing adoption drivers, vendor activity, limitations, and cited source URLs, with notes on evidence gaps.

output_examples.input: Compare three customer support platforms for a small SaaS team.
output_examples.output: A comparison organized by product, pricing signals, integration fit, operational tradeoffs, and a recommendation tied to the stated team needs.

output_examples.input: Prepare a report on regulatory changes affecting AI procurement.
output_examples.output: A saved research report with subtopic findings, source links, key requirements, open questions, and practical next steps.

best_practices: Define the research question and decision context before delegating subtopics.
Keep subtopics distinct so subagents do not duplicate the same searches.
Review cited sources and note uncertainty before using conclusions in important decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not use it for questions that only need one known fact.
Do not treat web source text as trusted instructions for the agent.
Do not let research folders accumulate sensitive or unrelated workspace data.

faq.question: Does this skill browse the web?
faq.answer: Yes. It instructs subagents to use web_search and can use fetch_url for pages when more detail is needed.

faq.question: Does it create files?
faq.answer: Yes. It creates a research folder and saves plans, findings, and optional reports in the workspace.

faq.question: Can it produce citations?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow asks subagents to include source URLs and uses those URLs during final synthesis.

faq.question: Is it suitable for sensitive research?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill uses web tools and local files, so avoid including secrets or private data in prompts.

faq.question: How many searches does it run?
faq.answer: The skill recommends three to five web searches per subtopic and up to three subagents in parallel.

faq.question: Does it verify every source independently?
faq.answer: No. It synthesizes gathered sources and can identify gaps, but users should verify important claims.
